speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Eli Epstein. I am taping Paul Strassmann. Today's date is May 12, 1995, and we are beginning the fourth tape. Please, continue and describe for me, if you will, what were the first episodes that you encountered? You've now teamed up with some Russians, and you've begun your partisan activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: The team I happened to bump into in a small town called Nova Lehota, which is about 15 kilometers southeast of Trencin, was a team that was headed by a professionally trained partisan who had previously been head of a partisan unit. And he was an expert, what's called a [NON-ENGLISH], somebody-- a mining expert. The team, at the time when I joined, it had about eight, eight members. There was only one other Russian. Two other members were people who were communists. One of them, the older man, he was in his 40s. There was a man who actually fought in the International Brigade in Spain, and he brought his son, 18-year-old son. It was-- His name was Tatko. His codename was Tatko, and he immediately took possession of the light machine gun. Then we had a young Czech refugee who escaped a camp, a prisoner camp. We had a young Ukrainian, also an escapee from a military transport of prisoners, Russian prisoners. And all of these were totally strange to, to Trencin. And they sort of questioned me, what I did and how I got around. And after telling my story, they said, well, you are just the kid we would like to have to walk ahead of the squad. The mission of that squad was to intercept a single rail line. The strategic position in the-- late in 1944 was that it wasn't clear yet whether the main attack of the Russians will come through the [? Vistula ?] or through the Danube Basin. And therefore the German High Command placed their reserves in a mobile status to be able to shuttle them across the Carpathian Mountains, which run east-west. The attack was either going to come through the south or through the north, so it was strategically important to keep those rail lines open because that's how the German army moved, rapidly with rail. There was not enough time to move a division, and it would have consumed too much fuel. The mission of this Bat'ko squad was basically to interdict the Trencin-Gilena main line, which continued then in Poland. And they were under strict orders not to engage in any kind of action with the Germans. The objective was to hit, strike, and disappear as quickly as possible. We had more than enough ammunition. We had more enough explosives. The problem was carrying the explosives, and they were very glad to use me as a carrier when I was not walking ahead of the squad as a way of leading them in the right direction. The objective was to find a part of the track where the track was on a curve and blow up one of the rails so that the weight, the heavy weight of the front end of the train-- which was usually in the tender, where the water was and the coal was stored-- would tilt the, the, the machine off the track and fall off an embankment, and thereby block the, the rail line. Clearly, we could not stop the traffic, but we surely could make it difficult and uncertain to have predicted troop movements over those lines because a loss of half a day or even a day in a key offensive when you are moving troops becomes a very critical element.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it would generally take a day or a half, something like a day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, depending how lucky you were and how much damage you did and how much did the front end of the train pull down the rest of the-- rest of the cars into the embankment really would, would, would really influence it. Of course, there was a second rail line, and the question was, you know, would, would you also block the traffic on the second line? So the geometry was very important. But Bat'ko was a very experienced man, and he was basically looking for places on a high embankment on a curve, not far from the edge of a forest, where you could come in and get out very quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a topographic map, and they said, well, is this a good place, or that place? And I knew how to read topographic maps. I suggested where we would go, and we proceeded shortly thereafter to a location near a town called Opatova where we arrived in a drizzling rain, marching mostly not during full daylight, but mostly early in the morning and before it got really dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: By that time, of course, the whole area was-- the mountains were subject to open fire. In other words, the Germans could shoot anybody they saw on site, so the, the, the big danger was, in fact, being detected by the peasants who saw us trooping by and who could inform on us. But we, by and large, travelled through, through, through the woods. Usually, I was in the front because I know the area going over the trail. I carried, initially, only two hand grenades, but I had a knapsack full of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] explosives, which were five kilo boxes made out of light steel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the first activity, the first mission you were sent on?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first mission was coming to the edge early in the morning. It was raining. We had some tents on us, and then staying on the edge, observing the traffic over the r-- over the lines, waiting for dusk because there was at least 500 yards, maybe more, between the edge of the forest and the-- and the embankment. Watching the traffic, not going out in daylight because the Germans usually had a machine gun nest on a flat car ahead of the engine and one behind. Waiting for the night, we set up two positions. A typical attack is that you set up flanking positions, and then the center crew goes forward, lays the mine, arms it, but does not attach it to the rail because it's the train. It's not, you know, pushing a plunger and blowing it up the way-- the way you see John Wayne movies. The partisan trick is to have the train do the explosion itself by place-- place-- placing the blasting cap on the outside flange of the rail so that when it gets hit, it gets ignited. And then you put in about a certain amount of cord in insufficient time, assuming the speed is about 20 kilometers an hour, that it would blow up halfway after the front wheel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you'd need to time in order to get to exactly--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, and this is why a professional is so useful. One of the tragedies of much of the partisan warfare that has been waged, both in Western Europe and in Eastern Europe, was that the professional know-how just wasn't there. And the, the conventional armies did not know how to do it. I mean, in those days, there was no such thing as special forces.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the decision was made to set up the machine guns on the flanking ends, and Bat'ko and I, another guy, were going to go in. I was going to carry the, the part of the load, and I was just there to help. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As soon as it got dark, Bat'ko climbs on the embankment. The 500 yards, by the way, between the edge of the forest and the rail track was freshly plowed field that had just been soaked by rain.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- between the fields, you usually have a path, so we went on the path, got to the embankment. And just as we start digging-- you take the gravel apart, and you then put the explosive under the rail. And then you take the cord and put it in front in the direction in which you expect the front wheel to hit it. And as we start laying it, we see in the distance the light of already of a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell from that light that it wasn't the passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a train was a train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it didn't matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't matter. The objective is to blow up something. The objective is to blow up the locomotives to block the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were being surprised of a train coming that quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't have a schedule and so forth. We just watched it, and the train came and fine. So in the process, I was given to hold certain pieces of the ignition, and, and I spilled it. Now, the, the caps were a key commodity. That was the rare thing-- you know, the blasting caps which trigger off the explosion. And I spilled it. And Bat'ko says, you better pick them up, and I did. I stuffed them in my pocket. These explosion caps, as you may know, on a slight-- you can actually with a finger explode them. They're-- they are picrate of mercury, so they're very easily exploded. We lay the thing, and Bat'ko takes off with his assistant and leaves me on the track to wait till the last moment to see if the Germans will come in with an advance, what's called a draisine. Sometimes, the Germans had a little car go ahead of the main train. Well, apparently this was not a military train, but it was a freight train, nevertheless. And it did have a machine gun nest, but in the middle because, you know, you get so many machine guns. And so after I see that there was no advance car going-- usually, what they have, they have a car going out with a search light shining on the tracks. And the tracks are sufficiently glistening that if anything lies on the tracks, you could see it, you know, yards and yards away. And usually they have some soldier, some disposal soldier, looking at this thing. There was none of them, so I just mounted the cap on the as I was taught, fastening it with tape. And the train was pretty close by that time. Really, I mean, you could almost smell it. It was that close. And I start running down the embankment. Panicking, unfortunately, I didn't find the path, so I was running over freshly plowed ground, which was lucky, by the way, because it was soft mud. But I couldn't run very fast. I just managed to go maybe 50 feet, maybe 40 feet, and just about over my shoulder when I-- the engine comes to where I expect it to blow. I hit the ground, and off it goes, just, just, you know, flying steam and metal parts all over the place, enormous amount of shouting. And in the middle of that freight train the Germans had on a flat car a sandbag, machine gun. It started immediately shooting flares, and it started raking the field with machine gun fire. It was pretty stupid. First, they were shooting too high because these were incendiary. So you could see that they were-- they were raking it too high. You see, they were on an embankment, so you don't-- haven't got the right angle. Although if you would have gotten further out, they would have had you in the line of fire. The second thing is when they shoot a flare, I don't know if you ever-- you know how-- you hear a pop before the flare gets up, so it takes about three seconds for the flare to get up and open the parachute. So all you have to do is when you hear a pop, you just go flat and just sit there, hoping that they don't discover you. Luckily-- again, just stupid luck-- the thing was so muddy, that I was so covered with mud that when I hit the ground, you know, apparently they didn't see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this went on for a while. Apparently, they were shooting high towards the edge of the forest because they felt that we must be hidden. They were raking the edge of the forest rather than the field, and this went on for quite a while. How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God, you know, like, 36 hours, so far as I'm concerned. [LAUGHS] I mean, I don't know how I made those 500 yards. By the way, I've been there since then. I've measured it and so forth, so I know pretty well the lay of the land. And, and the other luck was that because of the mud, the caps that I had in my pocket didn't blow up. I mean, it would have taken right off my foot. So ultimately the Germans just stop because, you know, they didn't see anything. I don't know what they assumed, or they ran out of ammo, or God knows what. They stopped raking the field.

speaker: interviewee
text: I climbed up to the forest and in due course, my guys, of course, pulled back. We always-- when you do a raid, you have what's called a primary position, and then you have an assembly position. They were at the assembly position. I got to the assembly position all caked up. Bat'ko says, where are the caps? The fact that I mounted the thing and took my life was not the issue. Where are the caps? And I-- right in my pocket. I said, here they are. Everybody pulled back and said, crazy kid, you know, because, we-- see, those caps are usually carried individually packed into a little pouch which is lined with cotton. So that's my, my story, which, of course, is recorded in the-- in the-- in the stories of Jewish partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: After that, I became a member of the-- of the troop. They gave me a carbine, which is a 792-millimeter, shorter gun, and I became a full-fledged member of the-- of the-- of the-- group. We were then assigned to a commander, a Russian commander by the name of Boginskii, who happened to be a beast, and we were just driven on more [INAUDIBLE]. So we just kept inching up ourselves up the line and see how much damage we could do. We did it until the 28th of October, when the Germans wiped out the fixed position of the Slovak perimeter. And then the Germans had enough forces. And these are mostly Ukrainian so-called Vlasov troops. These were not German Nazi troops. They were SS commanded, but they were basically Russian and mostly Ukrainian who turned to do German police actions for them. They are the same troop that, for instance, cleaned up Warsaw. They were used for bandit suppression. It was a very brutal [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] group of totally desperate individuals who, who really bargained their way out of the prison camp by turning out to be one of the most deadly anti-Jewish and anti-partisan groups. And they went hunting after us. They just hunted us. They actually hunted us at night. Until the 28th of October, we were fairly safe during the night. After that, we were not safe at all. And, of course, by November the leaves started falling down, so the only place we could really be protected against air-- they have these Fieseler Stork observation planes, which were used as spotters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we could only really stay in, in woods which were coniferous, which they provided cover. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, of course, winter came, November 15. By the way, I am going back to one of these places where we were surrounded. I talked to the military in Slovakia last week about making the visit to the battlegrounds, and they say, well, you better bring yourself some boots because there's still snow there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, this is May. There's still snow there. That will give you some idea where we operated. We really were being continually marginalized to higher, higher up, to recede further and further into the mountains where, where-- you know, there were no helicopters, no paratroops in those days. So we just withdrew further and further away from where we were reached because the lower ranges of the countryside after November 15 were, were totally occupied. And there was much treason going on and police informing of partisans who, who were captured that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any confrontations while you were retreating to higher altitudes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those weren't any rail-line type activities anymore?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we, we had-- you know, we cut down-- I, I don't think-- after October 28, I think we only did two raids. After that, it was just sheer survival. Particularly after December, it was just sheer survival. There was a mountain on which we were called [NON-ENGLISH] which is-- which is-- you know, you can barely survive there if you are well fed and well clothed, but we were on that mountain. And that's where I'm going to be next, yeah, Sunday night, a week from this coming Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you fed and clothed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the clothing was terrible. For instance, my shoes were pitiful. And no socks, so you wrapped your feet in, in, in just, like, bandages. I still have frostbite.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your toes? You still have frostbite to this day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, well, you know, damage by frostbite. And, and you got very easily infected. You see, there was no sulfa drugs, no nothing, so, so by and large when you rubbed your skin-- most of the time you were wet, and you did not have proper clothing or proper shoes. If your feet go rubbed, the skin got-- you know, gets soft and, you know, you could almost peel it off. And that got infected, so there was just lots of infection. Most of our casualties after November 15 were people just disease and frost and just give up, ghost. And they just couldn't march anymore. They just fell down, and don't pick me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- during the uprising, the estimated Jewish participation in the uprising was, like, 5%. After the liquidation of the uprising, the Jewish participation went up to 30% to 40%.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among the participants because the Jews had no place to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a kinship there at that point? In other words, would Jews who were among the partisans in a somewhat rather disproportionate number, did they somehow cling to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: In some respects. In some respects. I was lucky. We were-- you know, because of the casualties, we were continually reconfigured and recombined. You took straggle groups, and you created, you know, a new group. And these groups where usually not more than 30, 40 because that's how you survive, because you couldn't have too, too large of an organization. The group, the [? Yagerov ?] group that I joined after the Bat'ko group had two Jewish doctors, Elo Luja and Imre Blau. And they were very experienced. They were sort of fun guys, and, and they got some sulfa drugs. I don't know how they got them. And so they basically took care of my infections on my feet, which I think saved my life. Luja was a particular character. You know, the big problem was the nights got shorter after November, and it was freezing cold. Usually, you either were camping in some kind of a lean-to or in a dugout or into a hayrick. You could-- If you dug into a hayrick inside, particularly if the hayrick was not cleaned up for the winter-- because the peasants could go up to pick up the hay-- the hay was rotting. Rotted hay generates an enormous amount of heat. So you just dug yourself into the hay, OK, and then you stayed there. It's pretty warm. I mean, it can be freezing outside, but you can dig yourself into a hayrick and s-- or, or rotting straw is ideal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any particularly close friends or buddies during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I would say that, that Luja was a buddy. He sort of looked at me as, you know, this, this kid, this pitiful kid. You know, I always in this partisan scene, I was seen as the pitiful kid, you know? What is this 15-year-old kid doing in a place like this?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, everybody was older, older. You know, in other words, the-- for instance, Tatko, the communist from the Spanish Civil War, was in his-- in his late 40s, and most of the people who survived, you know, serious guys, were in their 20 and the 30s. So I was really the kid, the kid. And but I, I got the same rations as everybody else. Cigarettes were one of the key elements of ration. It was the currency-- liquor and cigarettes. And I always traded my cigarettes for bread. In one of my, my recollections about smoking is that here were people who had very little food, and they knew that if they don't eat, they will really be weak and they'll not be able to march because we continually marched. Yet they're trading bread and lard for cigarettes. And I said, well, that's one of the reasons I never want to smoke. So I always traded my cigarettes. We did raids on villages. We did raids on villages. We intercepted here and there some trucks. I mean, we were sort of a band.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I perfectly can understand the Croatians and the Serbs and all of those bands. I mean, we were a band. We were a 12th-century kind of a band of, of military desperadoes. I mean, there was no ideology or anything like that. It was survival, and how do we get food? And how can we protect ourselves, and how can we find a place where the Germans are not going to fight us?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pretty dismal kind of a survival existence. One of my favorite stories from that period is the, the Russians loved to send me into villages which were German occupied to get some food. Sometimes they were German occupied. Sometimes they were occupied by informers who called in, you know, the Ukrainians, who came in on motorcycles. And there are some stories about that. But one evening, I was sort of sent into a village. I go to the corner, corner [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] cottage, go in, and I say, you know, I'm a partisan. I want food. Well, we haven't got any food. And then I hear [SNORTING], you know, a pig snorting. I say, ah, a pig. You know, that's-- we want a pig. And the, the man says, you know, that's the only thing I have, you know, because you always kill the pig around Christmastime. That's the big protein intake for them. I mean, these are very poor peasants living up in the hollows of a God-forsaken country.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will continue. Did you get the pig?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you want me to continue?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pause here. Thank you.
